mission.title: Install and Configure Keda on Kubernetes
mission.description: KEDA is a Kubernetes-based Event Driven Autoscaling component that enables fine-grained autoscaling for event-driven workloads in Kubernetes. Installing KEDA allows your applications to scale based on external events, improving resource utilization and responsiveness.

steps.title: Add the KEDA Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official KEDA Helm repository to your local Helm setup with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add kedacore https://kedacore.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install KEDA using Helm
steps.description:
Install KEDA in your Kubernetes cluster with the following command, creating a namespace called 'keda':
```bash
helm install keda kedacore/keda --namespace keda --create-namespace --version 2.19.0
```

steps.title: Verify KEDA installation
steps.description:
Check that KEDA is running correctly by listing the pods in the 'keda' namespace:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace keda
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for KEDA
steps.description:
Update the KEDA deployment to set resource limits. Use the following command to edit the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment keda-operator --namespace keda
```
In the editor, add the following under 'spec.template.spec.containers.resources':
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi
  requests:
    cpu: 250m
    memory: 256Mi
```

steps.title: Set up RBAC for KEDA
steps.description:
Ensure that KEDA has the necessary permissions by applying the following RBAC configuration:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kedacore/keda/v2.19.0/deploy/rbac.yaml --namespace keda
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of KEDA will show the KEDA operator pod running in the 'keda' namespace. You can also verify that the resource limits and RBAC configurations are correctly applied by checking the deployment and roles.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kedacore https://kedacore.github.io/charts
helm repo update

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi
  requests:
    cpu: 250m
    memory: 256Mi

prerequisites.description: You need a running Kubernetes cluster and the Helm and kubectl command-line tools installed.
